title: Dr. Explains Why Retatrutide is KING of Fat Loss Peptides

segments.text: You want the scoop on Retatrutide? Why
it's everywhere, why your friend is on
it, where did they get it from, should
you try it, should you switch from
Tirzepatide, can you do just a little
and come off of it later, will you lose
muscle if you use it? Well, today I've
got all the answers to those questions
and more, so there's no room for
confusion, no holding back. And just as
a quick disclaimer, this is not medical
advice from me to you. I am just a
doctor on the internet, I am not your
doctor. So, first just for clarity's
sake, you need to know the difference
between the GLP-1 injectables and why
Retatrutide has become the king of
GLP-1s. The long and short of it is that
we now have good, better, best versions
of a weight loss peptide blend that not
only blunts your appetite and decreases
inflammation and insulin resistance, but
now increases your metabolism. It's a
trifecta, and the so-called best version
is Retatrutide. But why? Well, have you
ever been told that the more weight you
lose, the lower your metabolism will be?
Well, not with Retatrutide. It
effectively boosts your metabolic rate
while you lose fat in a caloric deficit.
Here's how it works. It's a peptide
blend of GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon
receptor agonism. In a nutshell, GLP-1
drugs hit GLP-1 receptors all over your
body, heavily in the brain and the gut.
This dumbs down your appetite and slows
down your digestion. Semaglutide is pure
GLP-1 receptor agonism, a peptide
designed to put the brakes on hunger
while constipating you and making you
nauseated. Then came Tirzepatide, a
combination of GLP-1 and GIP agonism,
glucose-dependent insulinotropic
polypeptide. GIP is complex, but it
essentially turns your fat from being
stubborn and inflamed into a healthy,
accessible fuel. It lowers inflammation
in fat, allowing you to become more
insulin sensitive and able to mobilize
fatty acids better. So, why is
tirzepatide so much better than
semaglutide? Because there are fewer
side effects and more weight loss with
that drug. And this is because research
suggests that GIP receptors in the brain
antagonize or dampen the nausea signals
triggered by pure GLP-1 agonism.
Essentially, tirzepatide hits that stop
eating button while simultaneously
pushing a don't throw up button.
Tirzepatide is amazing because it has
less GLP-1 in it than GIP agonism. See,
GIP is doing most of the heavy lifting.
Something we don't talk about enough
that's really cool is that hitting your
GIP receptors with using tirzepatide
causes a transient increase in energy
expenditure for no good reason at all.
We call it a futile calcium cycle,
meaning a calcium pump in your cells
gets activated and starts moving calcium
back and forth across the membranes for
no functional reason other than just to
consume energy. It's like revving your
engine while you're in neutral. It also
increases oxygen consumption in brown
fat, which also increases your resting
metabolic rate. So, using tirzepatide,
at least for the first few months,
increases your metabolism despite a
starvation effect due to less calories.
GIP receptor agonism also increases
blood flow to fat tissue, making it more
sensitive and open to breaking down.
With GLP-1, we went from 15% average
weight loss in the studies to 20 to 25%
weight loss in the studies on
tirzepatide or GLP-1 plus GIP. More
weight loss, less nausea, and a
revved-up metabolism. But let's shake
things up even more. Enter retatrutide.
The scientist who formulated this drug
deserves a prize, and he probably got
one. See, we really learned a lot with
this GIP to GLP-1 ratio. And retatrutide
still leans heavily on GIP receptor
agonism, but it adds a glucagon receptor
peptide to the mix. You can think of
glucagon is like a fat vacuum. In a
normal functioning human, glucagon is a
fasting hormone. It tells your liver to
release sugar when you're hungry,
basically to keep you from passing out.
But in retatrutide, because it's paired
with GLP-1 and GIP pushing insulin and
sugar into cells, the glucagon receptor
is then tricked or diverted into doing
its secondary jobs, which are lipolysis
or fat burning and thermogenesis.
Basically, it increases your metabolic
idle or your resting energy expenditure.
And this is cool. Glucagon does this
through a process called mitochondrial
uncoupling, essentially leaking protons
through the electron transport chain,
the factory assembly line we use to make
ATP or energy. So instead of making ATP,
you get energy released purely as heat.
The body literally starts wasting
calories as heat. And this is basically
just a metabolic cheat code. While GLP-1
and GIP are handling hunger and insulin
sensitivity, glucagon handles metabolic
output. It forces the body to stop being
stingy with its own fat stores. See,
normally when someone loses weight, they
can hit a point of starvation mode where
the body starts to fight weight loss,
lowering your metabolism in order to
match the lower caloric intake. But if
you have a glucagon signal, you prevent
the metabolic adaptation that usually
stalls weight loss. It tricks the body
into thinking it's in a high state of
energy demand, and it commands the body
to waste energy. It's kind of one of the
coolest and safest exercise in a bottle
versions we've ever seen. Obviously, it
can't replace actual exercise. But
speaking of safety, in a controlled
clinical setting, it's been proven to be
safe. But safe doesn't mean side effect
free. And because I know people are
getting their hands on this stuff, even
though it's not approved for human use
yet, and just using it however they
please, let me just go over what's been
noted so far as side effects. So, the
catches with retatrutide come down to
three areas. Number one, muscle loss.
What we've seen in the clinical trials
so far is that up to 40% of weight lost
on these GLP agonist peptide blends can
be lean mass, or muscle. 40%
That is 40% of your metabolic capacity.
And if you're like me, and you care
about muscle sticking around, and I know
you do, eat a minimum of 1 g per pound
of lean body mass that you have in
protein. And track your muscle mass, and
let it be a driver of your weight loss.
Losing weight fast is cool and all, but
if you lose 100 lb, and 40 of it is
muscle tissue, are you going to feel
good about that? So, here's where I'm
going to plug a product for you guys.
I'm a massive muscle nerd, because how
much muscle you have is directly tied to
how long you'll live, bearing any freak
accidents. So, I wanted to see if I
could find a product that you could use
at home to track your muscle mass. So,
about 3 weeks ago, I went and did a full
DEXA scan, and my results showed that I
have 20% body fat, and 96 lb of lean
tissue. I was actually pretty proud of
it. And then I got a Hum Health Body Pod
scale. It's like an InBody scale that
you can do from home. It took about 5
days for the measurements to learn my
body, but the Hum Body Pod literally
shows me at 20% body fat, and 96 lb of
lean tissue. I kid you not. It is the
same as the DEXA scan. So, I got Hum
Health to sponsor this video. Thank you,
Hum Health, and I got a discount code
for you to use. You can click on the
link in my description if you want to
track your muscle and fat mass from
home, especially if you're on a fat loss
or muscle gaining journey. And you can
use an HSA or FSA for Hum Body Pod if
you want. Be patient with fat loss. It
is not worth the muscle loss, but you
don't have to do it blind either. Other
things you need to be aware of should
you try retatrutide when it's approved
for human use of course, heart rate.
There's a natural pacemaker in your
heart that has glucagon receptors on it.
And so research is showing an increase
of 5 to 10 beats per minute in resting
heart rates. Now that could be
meaningful for someone with any sort of
risk of arrhythmia or tachycardia.
Allodynia. About 20% of the participants
in the studies on high doses, meaning 12
mg of retatrutide, reported a side
effect of allodynia where their skin
felt like it had been sunburned. It's
likely due to sensory neuron
sensitization. There are glucagon
receptors on sensory neurons and the
theory is that overloading these can
cause a lowered threshold for firing
sensory neurons. It may be only a
temporary thing however, meaning that it
goes away with slow titration of dosing.
And just like with semaglutide and
tirzepatide, the risk for pancreatitis
still exists, but in the clinical
trials, the incidence was less than 0.5%
and gallstones. The thing with
gallbladder issues is that rapid weight
loss is one of the top, if not the top
trigger for gallstones. So be aware of
that. Now again, a lot of people are
getting their hands on retatrutide.
Please don't ask me how to do that. I'm
not really allowed to say that on
YouTube, but I still see a ton of
questions around dosing and microdosing
GLPs. For retatrutide, in the trials,
the doses were 2 mg, 4 mg, 6, 9, and 12
mg spaced out in 4-week intervals,
meaning you have to be on one dose for 4
weeks before you can increase. And so
you can suppose that that's what's going
to be the recommended dosing. But
microdosing, I think people are doing
this to level out peaks of the
medication in their system, meaning if
you get really nauseous if you take a
GLP, maybe you could microdose it more
frequently throughout the week. Sounds
solid to me. But, I will say there's a
theoretical risk of receptor
desensitization
because incretin receptors like GLP-1
and GIP are meant to be pulsed by food
naturally. Some scientists would argue
that the dip at the end of a 7-day cycle
is actually a good thing. It might allow
your receptors a brief period of
breathing room to recycle back to the
cell surface. By microdosing and keeping
those receptors under 24/7 high-level
tension, you might actually accelerate
tolerance, meaning you'd need higher and
higher doses sooner to get the same
effect. I don't know. This is not fact.
It's just a theory I have. But, the life
cycle of these receptors does prove that
you can go on one of these GLP peptide
mixes for a certain period of time and
then come off of them. It's just that if
your metabolism is shot at the end of
it, you're going to gain weight pretty
quickly. A big question a lot of people
still have is why can't I get this? And
the answer is that retatrutide is still
in clinical trials. And why people have
it anyway? Well, they're not supposed
to. Everyone's using peptides that are
meant for research purposes only. It is
what it is. Retatrutide will be approved
for human use soon. We don't know when
exactly, but it's happening. I hope you
liked this video. If you did, please hit
like for me and subscribe to my channel.
I'm Dr. Ashley Frazey. Have the best
day.
